text.title: Scope Control Rules and Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Concrete rules for preventing scope creep, writing testable acceptance criteria, and operating the scope-guard agentic workflow.

rule.statement: Pair every approved scope change with a cost and schedule re-estimate in the same change request; reject standalone scope deltas that arrive without impact analysis.
rule.rationale: Scope without impact analysis is a blank check; teams accept scope and absorb the cost silently, which destroys schedule and budget discipline.

rule.statement: Run a scope creep retro monthly: review accepted "small" changes, count cumulative days added, present the aggregate to the sponsor.
rule.rationale: Small changes are invisible individually; aggregating them monthly makes the drift visible and creates a natural escalation trigger before it becomes a crisis.

rule.statement: When declining a scope request, offer an alternative (next phase, separate project) and document the decision; "no" with no alternative breeds work-arounds.
rule.rationale: Stakeholders who are rejected without alternatives find informal channels to get their request implemented anyway, bypassing scope control entirely.

rule.statement: Write acceptance criteria as testable assertions in Given/When/Then form, or as explicit numeric thresholds; reject criteria that are not independently verifiable.
rule.rationale: Narrative criteria ("the system should be fast") produce acceptance disputes; Gherkin or numeric criteria produce pass/fail verdicts that both parties can measure independently.

example.note: Acceptance criterion: "The search should return relevant results quickly." — Not testable; "relevant" and "quickly" are undefined.

Acceptance criterion: "Given a catalog of 10,000 products, when a user submits a search query, then results appear within 2 seconds and the top result matches the query keyword." — Independently verifiable.

rule.statement: Scope baseline lives in version-controlled YAML (scope/baseline.yaml); CODEOWNERS restricts direct edits to the sponsor; every baseline change requires an approved change-request ID in the commit message.
rule.rationale: Wiki-hosted scope documents have no diff history and no access control; git-tracked YAML makes changes auditable, and CODEOWNERS makes unauthorized edits impossible.

rule.statement: A scope-guard agent reviews PRs and new tickets against the WBS baseline and emits a verdict (in_scope / out_of_scope / ambiguous / gold_plating); humans merge — the agent never auto-approves.
rule.rationale: Agent verdicts remove routine triage labor; the human-in-loop on merge ensures political and contextual judgment is applied before scope boundaries are crossed.

antipattern.description: Locking scope baseline too early without adequate stakeholder validation, then defending it against legitimate clarifications.
antipattern.reason: Premature baseline lock traps the project in obsolete decisions; balance with disciplined change control that distinguishes clarifications (no cost/schedule impact) from scope additions (require approval).

antipattern.description: Gold plating — developers adding unrequested features to "improve" the product.
antipattern.reason: Gold plating consumes contingency, delays tested deliverables, and introduces unreviewed scope that may fail acceptance or create downstream support burden.
